stem: Evaluate \( \sin^{-1}\!\left( \sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \right) \), giving the value in the range of \( \arcsin \).
solution: \( \arcsin \) returns a value in \( [-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}] \). Reduce \( \sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \) to its sine value and take the angle in that range: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This returns the input angle itself, ignoring that \( \arcsin \) must land in \( [-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}] \).
This uses the supplement \( \pi - \theta \) rather than the principal value.
This has the wrong sign.
This differs from the principal value by a full \( \pi \).
This differs from the principal value by \( \pi \).
This uses the complementary angle instead of the principal value.

stem: Evaluate \( \cos^{-1}\!\left( \cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \right) \), giving the value in the range of \( \arccos \).
solution: \( \arccos \) returns a value in \( [0, \pi] \). Reduce \( \cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \) to its cosine value and take the angle in that range: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This returns the input angle itself, ignoring that \( \arccos \) must land in \( [0, \pi] \).
This adds a full \( \pi \) to the input.
This has the wrong sign; \( \arccos \ge 0 \).
This differs from the principal value by \( \pi \).
This uses the complementary angle instead of the principal value.
This uses the reflection \( 2\pi - \theta \) rather than the principal value.

stem: Evaluate \( \tan^{-1}\!\left( \tan\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \right) \), giving the value in the range of \( \arctan \).
solution: \( \arctan \) returns a value in \( (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}) \). Since \( \tan \) has period \( \pi \), reduce \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \) modulo \( \pi \) into that range: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This returns the input angle itself, ignoring that \( \arctan \) must land in \( (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}) \).
This uses \( \pi - \theta \) rather than reducing modulo \( \pi \).
This has the wrong sign.
This differs from the principal value by a full \( \pi \).
This differs from the principal value by \( \pi \).
This uses the complementary angle instead of the principal value.

stem: Evaluate \( \sin^{-1}\!\left( \cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \right) \), giving the value in the range of \( \arcsin \).
solution: By the co-function identity, \( \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=\cos\theta \), so \( \sin^{-1}(\cos\theta) \) equals \( \tfrac{\pi}{2}-\theta \) adjusted by a multiple of \( 2\pi \) to land in \( [-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}] \). Evaluating at \( \theta=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[d]]} \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This returns the input angle itself instead of applying the co-function relationship.
This adds \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \) instead of subtracting from it.
This has the wrong sign.
This differs from the principal value by a full \( \pi \).
This differs from the principal value by \( \pi \).
This has the wrong sign on the co-function reduction.

stem: What is the maximum value attained by \( g(x) = [[a]]\arccos(x) + ([[b]]) \) for \( x \) in the domain of \( \arccos \)?
solution: Since \( \arccos(x) \) ranges over \( [0,\pi] \) and \( [[a]]>0 \), \( g(x)=[[a]]\arccos(x)+([[b]]) \) is maximized when \( \arccos(x)=\pi \) (at \( x=-1 \)), giving \( g_{\max} = [[a]]\pi + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the midpoint of the range of \( \arccos \) instead of its maximum.
This has the wrong sign on the \( \pi \) term.
This subtracts the shift instead of adding it.
This drops the \( \arccos \) term entirely, reporting only the shift.
This uses the midpoint of the range and also subtracts the shift.
This double-counts the vertical shift.
